passages.paragraph: Mme Loisel was one of those pretty and charming girls who seem to be born, as if by an error of fate, into a family of clerks. She had no dowry, no expectations, no means of becoming known, understood, loved, and married by a man of wealth and distinction.
questions: How is Mme Loisel described in the paragraph?
What social class does Mme Loisel belong to?
What does the phrase ‘error of fate’ suggest about her birth?
What does Mme Loisel lack according to the paragraph?
What kind of life does she believe she deserves?

passages.paragraph: She was unhappy all the time, tortured by the feeling that she was made for all delicacies and all luxuries. She suffered from the poverty of her dwelling, from the shabbiness of the walls, from the worn-out chairs, and the ugliness of the curtains.
questions: Why was Mme Loisel unhappy all the time?
What kind of life did she feel she was made for?
What aspects of her house troubled her?
What does this paragraph reveal about her mindset?
How does her environment affect her happiness?

passages.paragraph: One evening her husband came home with an air of triumph, holding a large envelope in his hand. Inside was an invitation to a ball given by the Minister of Public Instruction.
questions: How did Mr Loisel appear when he came home?
What was inside the envelope?
Who was hosting the ball?
Why was the invitation important?
What change does this invitation bring to the story?

passages.paragraph: She threw the invitation on the table with annoyance, murmuring that she had nothing suitable to wear. She was distressed because she had no dress worthy of the occasion.
questions: How did Mme Loisel react to the invitation?
Why did she feel distressed?
What does this reaction show about her priorities?
What problem does she focus on immediately?
How does this paragraph reflect her character?

passages.paragraph: Mme Loisel borrowed a diamond necklace from her friend Mme Forestier. She was delighted and admired herself in the mirror, feeling proud and beautiful.
questions: From whom did Mme Loisel borrow the necklace?
How did the necklace make her feel?
What emotions are expressed in this paragraph?
Why was the necklace important to her?
What does this moment reveal about her personality?

passages.paragraph: She danced with delight, with passion, intoxicated with pleasure, thinking of nothing, in the triumph of her beauty and the success of her charm.
questions: How did Mme Loisel behave at the ball?
What made her feel intoxicated with pleasure?
What thoughts occupied her mind during the ball?
Which qualities brought her success at the ball?
What does this paragraph show about her desires?

passages.paragraph: When they returned home, Mme Loisel discovered that the necklace was missing. She was terrified and overcome with fear.
questions: What discovery did Mme Loisel make after the ball?
How did she react to the loss?
What emotions are highlighted in this paragraph?
Why was the loss of the necklace serious?
How does this moment change the story’s direction?

passages.paragraph: The Loisels decided to replace the necklace. They borrowed money and lived a life of extreme poverty, working hard to repay the debt for ten long years.
questions: What decision did the Loisels make?
How did they arrange money to replace the necklace?
What kind of life did they live afterward?
How long did their suffering last?
What does this paragraph reveal about consequences?

passages.paragraph: Mme Loisel became a woman of strong will, rough and hard. She did heavy housework and learned the odious work of the kitchen.
questions: How did Mme Loisel change over the years?
What kind of work did she begin to do?
What qualities did hardship develop in her?
How is her transformation described?
What caused this change in her life?

passages.paragraph: Mme Forestier told her that the necklace was false and worth very little. Mme Loisel was shocked to learn that her years of suffering were caused by a fake necklace.
questions: What truth does Mme Forestier reveal?
How valuable was the necklace actually?
How does Mme Loisel react to the revelation?
Why is this moment considered ironic?
What does this paragraph reveal about the theme of the story?
